title: About
text:
I am a medical doctor and doctoral candidate (Dr. med.) affiliated with two institutions at Heidelberg University. My primary research is conducted at BioQuant, Heidelberg University under the supervision of Prof. Dr. Vytautė Starkuvienė-Erfle, while my doctoral degree is registered at the Medizinische Fakultät Mannheim under Prof. Dr. med. Michael Keese, who oversees my research project, funding, and thesis. The formal Doktorvater is PD Dr. med. Martin Sigl.
My work bridges clinical vascular medicine and computational biology, focusing on the molecular mechanisms underlying VSMC phenotypic plasticity.
Before my doctoral studies, I worked as a cardiovascular surgeon at the Third Affiliated Hospital of Chongqing Medical University (2020–2021), where I gained clinical experience in vascular and endovascular procedures.

title: Research
text:
Vascular smooth muscle cells (VSMCs) are highly plastic cells capable of transitioning between a quiescent contractile phenotype and a proliferative synthetic phenotype — a switch central to the pathogenesis of atherosclerosis and in-stent restenosis.
My primary doctoral project investigates the transcriptional basis of this switch through an integrated computational–experimental framework:

Discovery — Donor-aware modelling of public human aortic SMC transcriptomic data to delineate a phenotypic switching program.
Transferability — Evaluation of the program across independent perturbation contexts (growth-factor, cytokine, and metabolic/stress stimuli).
Human plaque anchoring — Cross-referencing with published human atherosclerotic plaque single-cell datasets.
Experimental validation — Perturbation models in primary human aortic SMCs (HAoSMC), paired with pharmacological counter-stimuli to test the functional identity of the program.

Key tools: R (limma, WGCNA, clusterProfiler, GSVA), Western Blot, Immunofluorescence, RT-qPCR.

title: Research
text:
ABCB5⁺ MSCs & Chronic Wound Healing
ABCB5⁺ MSCs are a rare population of skin-derived stromal cells defined by expression of the ATP-binding cassette transporter ABCB5, with immunomodulatory and tissue-regenerative properties.
This line of research has two components:
Published review — A comprehensive review of the differentiation and regeneration potential of ABCB5⁺ MSCs and their clinical translation perspectives, published in Journal of Clinical Medicine (2025).
Original bioinformatics study — A systems-level multi-modal analysis of the paracrine role of ABCB5⁺ MSCs in chronic wound repair, integrating:

Expression-based paracrine candidate identification
Chronic wound transcriptome profiling (co-expression network analysis)
Single-cell RNA-seq contextualization and cell–cell communication analysis
Immune micro-environment profiling
Causal-inference and structural plausibility analyses

Computational findings are complemented by experimental co-culture studies examining the paracrine effects of ABCB5⁺ MSCs on wound-relevant cell populations. Manuscript in preparation.

title: Research
text:
Endocytic Trafficking in HeLa Cells
Working within the Starkuvienė Lab at BioQuant, I investigate endocytic pathway regulation in HeLa Kyoto cells using:

Size-selective dextran-FITC uptake assays — fluid-phase endocytosis quantification across molecular-weight ranges
Pathway-specific pharmacological inhibition — dissecting macropinocytic and dynamin-dependent endocytic contributions
3D spheroid culture models — ultra-low-attachment platforms for 3D-context validation
Wound healing migration assays — scratch assay with quantitative imaging analysis

This work contributes to the lab’s broader programme on the spatial and temporal regulation of intracellular trafficking and its dysregulation in disease and aging.

title: Research
text:
Methods Overview







Category
Techniques




Bioinformatics
Bulk RNA-seq (limma, edgeR), scRNA-seq (Seurat), WGCNA, GSVA, GSEA, Mendelian Randomization (TwoSampleMR, coloc), CellChat, ssGSEA, molecular docking (HDOCK)


Wet Lab
Western Blot, Immunofluorescence, RT-qPCR, Wound Healing Assay, Cell Viability (CTB), Dextran Uptake Assay, 3D Spheroid Culture


Imaging
Widefield fluorescence microscopy, Confocal microscopy (Z-stack), Quantitative image analysis (ImageJ)


Cell Models
Primary human aortic SMCs (HAoSMC), HeLa cells, ABCB5⁺ MSCs


Languages
R, Python

title: Zheng He
text:
Doctoral Candidate (Dr. med.)
🏛 Starkuvienė Lab, BioQuant, Heidelberg University · Heidelberg 🏥 Medizinische Fakultät Mannheim, Heidelberg University · Mannheim
Supervised by Prof. Dr. med. Michael Keese & Prof. Dr. Vytautė Starkuvienė-Erfle Doktorvater: PD Dr. med. Martin Sigl

✉ zheng.he@bioquant.uni-heidelberg.de ✉ hezheng.surgeon@gmail.com 🎓 Google Scholar ORCID GitHub
My doctoral research spans three interconnected lines of investigation, combining computational transcriptomics, quantitative imaging, and experimental cell biology.
VSMC phenotypic plasticity — My primary project investigates the transcriptional basis of vascular smooth muscle cell (VSMC) phenotypic switching — the transition between a quiescent contractile state and a proliferative synthetic state central to atherosclerosis and in-stent restenosis. The work combines donor-aware computational analyses of public human aortic SMC transcriptomic data with experimental validation in primary human aortic SMCs, integrating perturbation transcriptomics, single-cell anchoring to human plaque states, and wet-lab perturbation models to interrogate the functional identity of the switching program.
ABCB5⁺ MSCs & chronic wound healing — A second line of research concerns ABCB5⁺ MSCs — a rare population of skin-derived stromal cells with immunomodulatory and regenerative properties. Beyond a published review (Journal of Clinical Medicine, 2025), I am leading a multi-modal bioinformatics study integrating expression-based paracrine candidate identification, chronic wound transcriptome profiling, single-cell contextualization, cell-cell communication analysis, and causal-inference methods, complemented by experimental co-culture studies of ABCB5⁺ MSC paracrine effects on wound-relevant cell populations.
Endocytic trafficking in HeLa cells — Working within the Starkuvienė Lab, I investigate endocytic pathway regulation in HeLa Kyoto cells using size-selective dextran-FITC uptake assays, pathway-specific pharmacological inhibition dissecting macropinocytic and dynamin-dependent endocytic contributions, 3D spheroid culture models, and wound healing migration assays. This work contributes to the lab’s programme on the spatial and temporal regulation of intracellular trafficking and its dysregulation in disease and aging.

title: Zheng He
text:
Research Interests


Vascular Biology
VSMC phenotypic plasticity, contractile-to-synthetic transition, atherosclerosis, in-stent restenosis


Computational Biology
Bulk RNA-seq, scRNA-seq, WGCNA, GSVA, GSEA, Mendelian randomization, cell-cell communication analysis, molecular docking


Stem Cell & Wound Biology
ABCB5⁺ MSC paracrine biology, chronic wound repair, stem cell differentiation, endocytic pathway regulation, intracellular trafficking
